content.description:
A Hob view pattern for building interactive UIs with local state that update efficiently without triggering kernel re-renders.

## The Problem

Hobson's rendering system re-evaluates a view's entire Hob code to produce a new DOM tree, then uses morphdom to diff it against the existing DOM. This works well for data-driven views, but is expensive and lossy for views with rich local state:

- **Expensive**: Re-evaluation means re-querying all items from the database, rebuilding all data structures, and re-running all logic — even if only one tab changed.
- **Lossy**: Even with morphdom diffing, a full re-render resets local state that lives outside the DOM: atom values, scroll positions in sibling panels, text in filter inputs, focus state.
- **Circular**: If the view uses atoms for local state (e.g. which tab is selected), and atom mutations trigger re-renders, the view re-renders itself infinitely.

## The Solution: Build Once, Update Surgically

Split the view into two phases:

1. **Skeleton** — Use `hiccup->dom+` to create a stable outer DOM structure with named refs. This runs once, at initial evaluation.
2. **Builders** — Define functions that use `dom-set-content!` to replace the children of specific ref'd containers. Call these imperatively when local state changes.

```hob
;; Phase 1: Stable skeleton (runs once)
(let [state (atom "tab-a")
      [root refs] (hiccup->dom+
        [:div {}
          [:div {:ref :tabs}]
          [:div {:ref :content}]])
      tabs-el (:tabs refs)
      content-el (:content refs)

      ;; Phase 2: Builder functions
      build-tabs! (fn []
        (dom-set-content! tabs-el
          (for [t ["tab-a" "tab-b"]]
            [:div {:style (if (= t (deref state)) "font-weight:bold" "")
                   :on-click (fn [e]
                               (reset! state t)
                               (build-tabs!)
                               (build-content!))}
              t])))
      build-content! (fn []
        (dom-set-content! content-el
          [:div {} (str "Showing: " (deref state))]))]

  ;; Initial render
  (build-tabs!)
  (build-content!)
  root)
```

## Why Not Morphdom?

Morphdom is the kernel's tool for diffing between full re-renders. It operates at the wrong level for intra-view updates:

| Concern | Morphdom (kernel) | dom-set-content! (view) |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------------|
| Trigger | Kernel calls `rerenderItem` | View calls builder imperatively |
| Scope | Entire view DOM tree | Single container's children |
| Cost | Re-evaluate all Hob code, re-query DB | Rebuild one panel's hiccup |
| State | Resets atoms, closures | Preserves atoms, sibling state |
| Diffing | Tree diff old vs new | Replace children wholesale |

The key insight is that `dom-set-content!` doesn't diff — it clears and replaces children of a specific container. This is efficient because each builder only touches a small subtree, and sibling containers (with their scroll positions, input values, etc.) are untouched.

## Forward-Declared Builders

Because builder functions often call each other (clicking a tab rebuilds both the tab bar and the content), and Hob's `let` bindings are sequential, use atoms for forward declaration:

```hob
(let [build-tabs!    (atom nil)
      build-content! (atom nil)

      _ (reset! build-tabs!
          (fn [] ... ((deref build-content!)) ...))

      _ (reset! build-content!
          (fn [] ...))
  ...)
```

This is necessary because `build-tabs!` needs to call `build-content!`, which isn't defined yet at the point `build-tabs!` is defined.

## Self-Owned Atoms

For this pattern to work, local atom mutations must NOT trigger a kernel re-render. The evaluator tags atoms with their creation context (`_ownerCtx`), and the rendering system skips dependency tracking for self-owned atoms. This means `(reset! state "tab-b")` inside an event handler updates the atom's value without the kernel knowing — exactly what we want.

If this mechanism breaks, the symptom is an infinite re-render loop: atom reset triggers re-render, re-render re-creates atoms, which triggers another re-render.

## When to Use This Pattern

- **Multi-panel browsers** with selection state (system-browser-view)
- **Tab interfaces** where switching tabs shouldn't re-query everything
- **Filtered lists** where typing in a filter should only update the list, not the whole view
- **Any view with local interactive state** that changes frequently

For simple views that just display data and have no local state, a plain hiccup return (letting the kernel handle re-renders via morphdom) is simpler and sufficient.

## Real-World Example

The [system-browser-view](item://c61afb35-cd78-4122-a9ff-7e28b884c74e) is the canonical example. It has:
- 4 atoms: `axis`, `col1-sel`, `col2-tab`, `col2-sel`
- 4 builders: `build-tab-bar!`, `build-col1!`, `build-col2!`, `build-detail!`
- A stable skeleton with refs for each panel
- Persistent axis selection via `get-view-config` / `update-view-config!`

## See Also

- [Silent Update Pattern](item://c0c0c0c0-0010-0000-0000-000000000000) — The kernel-level counterpart: saving data without re-rendering
- [Scroll State Preservation](item://c0c0c0c0-0012-0000-0000-000000000000) — An older JS pattern for the same underlying problem
- [Common Code Gotchas](item://c0c0c0c0-0016-0000-0000-000000000000) — Other pitfalls to watch for
